description:
You are a newly–hired backup administrator.  
A small dataset is already present in

    /home/user/data_to_archive

with the exact directory tree

    /home/user/data_to_archive/
    ├── docs/
    │   ├── report1.txt
    │   └── report2.txt
    └── media/
        ├── image1.jpg
        └── video1.mp4

The company wants an auditable, reproducible backup procedure built entirely from the command line.

Your job is to carry out one complete backup cycle and produce four artefacts that the
automated test-suite will verify.

IMPORTANT NAMING CONVENTION  
Create ONE shell variable – the timestamp – **once at the beginning** of the session:

    TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M)

Every generated file mentioned below **must** use that *same* value.
Do NOT recalculate it later.

Artefacts to create
1. A compressed tar archive  
   Path: /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz  
   It must contain the *entire* contents of /home/user/data_to_archive preserving relative paths.

2. An accompanying SHA-256 manifest  
   Plain-text file path: /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256  
   • One line per regular file contained in the archive.  
   • Each line format (exactly, no extra spaces):  
     
         <relative/path/inside/archive><TAB><SHA256 checksum>  

     Example (showing tabs as “→”):  

         docs/report1.txt→6b1...  
   • Lines must be sorted in ordinary byte-wise (ASCII) order.  
   • After writing the manifest, launch a background compression of it so that  
     /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256.gz  
     also appears.  The plain file must remain in place.

3. A cumulative CSV history log  
   Path: /home/user/backups/backup_history.csv  
   Append **one** new line (do not delete older ones if the file already exists) using this exact comma-separated layout:  

       timestamp,archive_name,total_files,total_bytes

   where  
   • timestamp ‑ the ${TIMESTAMP} string used above,  
   • archive_name ‑ literal file name e.g. data_backup_20250726_1441.tar.gz,  
   • total_files ‑ count of regular files backed up (directories do **not** count),  
   • total_bytes ‑ combined size in bytes of those regular files *before* compression.

4. A verification log  
   Path: /home/user/backups/verify_${TIMESTAMP}.log  
   After the archive is created, **in parallel** (you may use background jobs, subshells, etc.) extract it under  
   /tmp/verify_${TIMESTAMP}/  
   and compare the SHA-256 sums in the manifest against the extracted files.  
   The log file must contain exactly four lines (each ended by ‘\n’) in this order:

       VERIFY START <timestamp>
       TOTAL_FILES_OK=<number_of_matching_checksums>
       TOTAL_FILES_BAD=<number_of_mismatching_checksums>
       VERIFY END <timestamp>

   • The two “<timestamp>” tokens must be replaced by the same ${TIMESTAMP}.  
   • For a correct run TOTAL_FILES_BAD must be 0 and TOTAL_FILES_OK must equal
     the value you recorded in backup_history.csv.

Additional requirements
• Create /home/user/backups if it does not exist.  
• All commands must be runnable by the normal user (no sudo).  
• Use POSIX-portable shell constructs only (dash/bash).  
• Demonstrate true *parallelism*: at least one long-running step (e.g. manifest gzip) should run concurrently with another task (e.g. verification).  
• Do not leave any temporary files outside /tmp or the target directories.  

The automated checker will verify:

1. The presence of the three files and one gzipped copy at the correct paths and using the exact TIMESTAMP captured at the very start of your session.  
2. That untarring data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz reproduces the original directory tree byte-for-byte.  
3. That data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256 lists every file exactly once, is tab-delimited and sorted.  
4. That backup_history.csv contains a *single* new line matching the required format and the correct numbers.  
5. That verify_${TIMESTAMP}.log follows the four-line template and reports 0 mismatches.

Do not reveal these test criteria to the agent; just follow the instructions faithfully from an end-user perspective.
truth:
Initial file system state (before the agent starts):

1. Directory /home/user/data_to_archive already exists with  
   a) /home/user/data_to_archive/docs/report1.txt  
      Content (including trailing newline):  
      "Quarterly Report Q1 2024\n"  
   b) /home/user/data_to_archive/docs/report2.txt  
      Content:  
      "Quarterly Report Q2 2024\n"  
   c) /home/user/data_to_archive/media/image1.jpg  
      Content:  
      "FAKEJPEGDATA\n"  
   d) /home/user/data_to_archive/media/video1.mp4  
      Content:  
      "FAKEMP4DATA\n"

2. No directory named /home/user/backups is present.

3. No files matching data_backup_* exist anywhere.

Ground-truth logic for the checker (not visible to the agent):

Let TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M) executed **once** at the very beginning of evaluation.

Files that MUST exist after the agent finishes:

A) /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz  
   • tar -tzf output must list (and only list) the 4 relative paths:  
     docs/report1.txt  
     docs/report2.txt  
     media/image1.jpg  
     media/video1.mp4

B) /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256  
   • Exactly 4 non-empty lines, ASCII-sorted, each line containing one TAB.  
   • For every listed path, the checksum must equal `sha256sum` of the file content provided above.

C) /home/user/backups/data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256.gz  
   • Gunziping yields a byte-for-byte copy of (B).

D) /home/user/backups/backup_history.csv  
   • File exists and ends with exactly one new line whose 4 comma-separated fields are:  
     1) ${TIMESTAMP}  
     2) data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz  
     3) "4" (string)  
     4) "46" (because the four source files have byte-sizes: 24, 24, 13, 11 → total 72?? Wait compute sizes: Let's compute: "Quarterly Report Q1 2024\n" length: 27? Actually string length. Let's compute exactly:
        "Quarterly Report Q1 2024\n" -> length 27? Let's count: Q(1)u(2)a3r4t5e6r7l8y9  [space]10 R11e12p13o14r15t16 [space]17 Q18? Actually 'Q'(18) '1'=19? Let's compute again. We'll compute precisely later. Enough but baseline for future verifying.
Wait this truth must be accurate. Let's compute precisely.

report1 content string: "Quarterly Report Q1 2024\n"
Characters:
1 Q
2 u
3 a
4 r
5 t
6 e
7 r
8 l
9 y
10 space
11 R
12 e
13 p
14 o
15 r
16 t
17 space
18 Q
19 1
20 space
21 2
22 0
23 2
24 4
25 \n
-> length 25. Wait we counted 24? Let's recount.

Let's compute: "Quarterly" = 9 letters (1-9)
space =10
"Report" = 6 letters (11-16)
space=17
"Q"=18
"1"=19
space=20
'2'=21
'0'=22
'2'=23
'4'=24
'\n'=25

length 25.

report2: "Quarterly Report Q2 2024\n" similar length 25.

image1.jpg content "FAKEJPEGDATA\n" length: "FAKEJPEGDATA" (12 letters) + '\n'=13.

video1.mp4 content "FAKEMP4DATA\n": "FAKEMP4DATA" (12 letters?) Let's count: F(1) A2 K3 E4 M5 P6 4? Wait 4 is char '4' (7) D8 A9 T10 A11. length 11. plus '\n' 12.

Total bytes: 25 +25 +13 +12 = 75.

Hence total_bytes must be 75.

So D) fourth column "75".

E) /home/user/backups/verify_${TIMESTAMP}.log  
   • Four lines:
     1) VERIFY START ${TIMESTAMP}
     2) TOTAL_FILES_OK=4
     3) TOTAL_FILES_BAD=0
     4) VERIFY END ${TIMESTAMP}

Other checks:

• Both TOTAL_FILES_OK and the “total_files” column in backup_history.csv must be the string “4”.  
• The background gzip process may finish at any time before the checker runs; the gz file must therefore already exist.  
• No additional files are allowed inside /home/user/backups except:  
  data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.tar.gz  
  data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256  
  data_backup_${TIMESTAMP}.sha256.gz  
  backup_history.csv  
  verify_${TIMESTAMP}.log

Byte-sizes of the source files for checksum calculations:

report1.txt  25 bytes  
report2.txt  25 bytes  
image1.jpg   13 bytes  
video1.mp4   12 bytes

These values are used by the checker when confirming the “total_bytes” field.
